Title: Manipulation of the Xanthophyll Cycle Increases Plant Susceptibility to Sclerotinia sclerotiorum

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: PsbS family, PSII subunit S protein
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of PsbS greatly attenuated the Sclerotinia-induced NPQ increase, supporting requirement of PsbS-dependent NPQ for the pathogen-manipulated xanthophyll-cycle response.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. npq4-1 plants showed strongly reduced abnormal NPQ induction after Sclerotinia infection. 2. Most residual NPQ in npq4-1 relaxed poorly, consistent with defective qE. 3. PsbS is required for full pathogen-induced NPQ activation linked to altered xanthophyll-cycle metabolism.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis (npq4-1); chlorophyll fluorescence NPQ kinetics after Sclerotinia inoculation
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Sclerotinia sclerotiorum infection; low light or actinic light illumination
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: PsbS is not the direct focus for metabolite accumulation, but is an experimentally tested core component required for the manipulated NPQ phenotype that connects lumen acidification to zeaxanthin-dependent energy dissipation and downstream ABA effects.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Zeaxanthin supports photoprotection/energy dissipation; ABA promotes defense responses including ROS induction and callose deposition against Sclerotinia.

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: AP2/ERF family transcription factor-like ABA signaling component
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense signaling, ROS induction, callose deposition, stomatal regulation
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Defects in ABI4 increased susceptibility despite the npq1-2 background, indicating ABA signaling downstream of xanthophyll-cycle effects is required for resistance.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. npq1-2/abi4-1 plants were more susceptible to Sclerotinia than npq1-2. 2. Exogenous ABA did not rescue the susceptibility of npq1-2/abi4-1. 3. ABI4 acts downstream of ABA in defense against Sclerotinia.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Double-mutant disease assays; ABA rescue test; lesion area measurement
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because it is directly tested in Results as a core downstream ABA signaling component connecting altered xanthophyll metabolism to defense output, although not itself a metabolic enzyme.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss of ABI4 compromises disease resistance.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: NCED3 transcript increased upon infection, likely as a compensatory response to reduced ABA precursor availability rather than the primary cause of ABA decline.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Among tested ABA biosynthetic genes, NCED3 was the notable transcript induced after Sclerotinia challenge. 2. ABA still decreased in wild-type Sclerotinia-infected tissue. 3. The study interprets NCED3 induction as compensation for precursor limitation caused by xanthophyll-cycle manipulation.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Wild-type Sclerotinia versus oxalate-deficient A2 infection
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Not designated as primary causal core driver of the metabolite phenotype; included because it was directly profiled in Results and discussed as a compensatory ABA-biosynthesis response.

Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: npq1-2/aba2-3 double mutant; Col-0 for expression assay
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Short-chain dehydrogenase/reductase involved in ABA biosynthesis
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense signaling, ROS induction, callose deposition
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of ABA2 increased susceptibility in the npq1-2 background, and exogenous ABA rescued the phenotype, showing ABA biosynthesis is required for resistance downstream of xanthophyll-cycle manipulation.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ABA2 transcript was not significantly changed by Sclerotinia in qPCR. 2. npq1-2/aba2-3 plants were more susceptible than npq1-2. 3. ABA treatment rescued npq1-2/aba2-3 susceptibility, confirming ABA deficiency as the cause.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: qRT-PCR; double-mutant disease assay; ABA rescue test
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Wild-type Sclerotinia and A2 mutant infection; exogenous ABA pretreatment
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Used genetically to prove the importance of ABA biosynthesis in the disease phenotype caused by xanthophyll-cycle manipulation.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: De-epoxidizes violaxanthin to antheraxanthin and then to zeaxanthin under low lumen pH.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step for zeaxanthin-dependent NPQ in this study
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Diverts xanthophyll precursors away from ABA precursor pool and toward zeaxanthin accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Zeaxanthin mediates photoprotection/NPQ; ABA supports defense responses including ROS induction and callose deposition
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Activation of VDE during Sclerotinia infection increases zeaxanthin and NPQ while decreasing violaxanthin/neoxanthin availability for ABA biosynthesis, thereby enhancing susceptibility.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. npq1-2 mutation abolished the Sclerotinia-induced NPQ increase. 2. DTT inhibition of VDE strongly inhibited pathogen-induced NPQ. 3. HPLC showed increased zeaxanthin and decreased violaxanthin/neoxanthin in infected Col-0 but not npq1-2. 4. npq1-2 attenuated the ABA decrease caused by Sclerotinia and reduced lesion size, linking VDE activity to susceptibility.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Loss-of-function mutant (npq1-2); chemical inhibition by DTT; HPLC pigment profiling; ABA quantification; disease lesion assays
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Sclerotinia sclerotiorum infection; low light; DTT treatment; oxalate-mediated acidification
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Function assayed in infected Arabidopsis leaves
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Reducing VDE activity in crop contexts may preserve violaxanthin/neoxanthin and ABA-dependent defense against Sclerotinia, but may compromise photoprotection under fluctuating/high light; deployment would require balancing disease resistance and photosynthetic stress tolerance.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Central metabolic switch in the paper: pathogen-triggered acidification activates VDE, deepening xanthophyll de-epoxidation and restricting ABA precursor supply.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: VDE suppression is expected to weaken NPQ photoprotection and may increase light stress sensitivity.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: aba1-3; Ler-0 comparison in supplemental data; Col-0 qPCR primer set
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Epoxidizes zeaxanthin via antheraxanthin to violaxanthin, replenishing ABA precursor and reversing xanthophyll deepoxidation.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not established in this paper as rate-limiting; used as contextual comparator
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies precursor pool for ABA biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Referenced through the aba1-3 mutant as a comparator showing constitutive zeaxanthin accumulation and NPQ dynamics similar to infected tissue, supporting the conclusion that zeaxanthin accumulation underlies the pathogen-induced NPQ phenotype.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Sclerotinia-infected tissue showed NPQ dynamics similar to aba1-3. 2. This comparison supports a role of elevated zeaxanthin in the altered NPQ kinetics. 3. ABA1/ZEP also provides the violaxanthin precursor pool required for ABA formation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative NPQ kinetics using aba1-3 supplemental analysis; qRT-PCR primer usage for ABA1
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Not a primary manipulated gene in Results; included because aba1-3 comparison is explicitly used to interpret the core metabolic phenotype.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss of ZEP would increase zeaxanthin and NPQ-related effects while reducing ABA precursor regeneration.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: qRT-PCR; supplemental NPQ phenotyping; no accession reported

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Genetic disruption of ABA2 increased susceptibility, showing that intact ABA biosynthesis is required for the resistance phenotype preserved in npq1-2.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. aba2 mutation in the npq1-2 background increased susceptibility to wild-type Sclerotinia and A2 mutant. 2. ABA treatment rescued the npq1-2/aba2-3 phenotype. 3. ABA2 is functionally required for ABA-mediated defense output in this system.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Double-mutant disease assay; exogenous ABA rescue
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although also listed as a common/core genetic component, its biochemical role is enzymatic in ABA biosynthesis.
